title: Open Workshop: Project Management, Scaling, Scheduling, and Cost Plus Contracts
summary: Participants brought a wide range of live business challenges to this open workshop, covering topics including what makes a great project manager, when and how to scale staff, client communication around scheduling changes, warranty handover processes, cost plus contract structures with owner-supplied materials, and systems for training green hires. Erin drew heavily on her production home building background to offer practical frameworks and real-time examples from her own active projects.

teachings.concept: The Two Traits of a Great Project Manager: Urgency and Attention to Detail
teachings.explanation: Regardless of background, the most effective project managers share two qualities: they treat everything as urgent and they insist on facts rather than opinions or approximations. These traits can be trained but must be consistently reinforced by owners through daily presence and accountability.
teachings.quote: The common thread is that all of them understand that the details count. And that's down to every little detail counts.

teachings.concept: Know Your Position Before You Hire
teachings.explanation: Before adding any staff, owners must have a clear, visible 12-month cash flow picture. This financial positioning informs whether there is enough work to justify payroll and guides the urgency with which owners pursue new projects.
teachings.quote: The number one way to make it rain is to talk directly to our feeders. And that doesn't mean email. That means getting in front of them and figuring out how to work with them.

teachings.concept: Hire Experts with an Owner Mindset
teachings.explanation: When scaling, Erin looks for two things in every hire: deep expertise in a specific function, and an owner mindset — someone who takes full responsibility for their work without needing to be chased. Setting clear ground rules, such as empowering someone to make a thousand-dollar decision independently, can help cultivate this mindset.
teachings.quote: I can't stand it when I have to chase people to do the work I hired them to do. It drives me bonkers and it won't last in my company.

teachings.concept: The Two-Week Rolling Calendar as a Training and Execution Tool
teachings.explanation: A pencil-and-paper two-week calendar reviewed at the start and end of each week builds discipline, forces planning before doing, and wires the schedule into a project manager's thinking. Crumpling and restarting the calendar weekly reinforces the habit and keeps the process active rather than passive.
teachings.quote: He would go through the two week calendar with me. He would teach me the methodology behind what needs to happen and why. And then he would say, go out and do this stuff.

teachings.explanation: Construction cannot be run in reaction mode. Every task must follow a sequence: plan first, execute second, verify third. Skipping the planning step causes the inefficiencies and scrambles that bleed money and credibility.
teachings.quote: Before we do anything, step one is plan. Can't move to step B until we plan.

teachings.explanation: The builder's role is to present accurate information — costs, timelines, trade-offs — and then let the homeowner decide. When clients are kept fully informed and make their own choices, they cannot blame the builder for outcomes. This mindset also requires proactive, sometimes uncomfortable phone calls rather than silence.
teachings.quote: My entire goal is they need to own their decisions. We're here to execute.

teachings.concept: Delivering an Exceptional Home Before Turnover Minimizes Warranty Pain
teachings.explanation: The goal at project closeout is to hand over a home with the smallest possible punch list. This requires a rigorous internal walk before the client walk, giving clients breathing room after move-in to compile a list, and maintaining ongoing personal relationships so small issues are handled naturally rather than adversarially.
teachings.quote: When you turn that house over, your entire goal is to have the smallest list possible.

teachings.concept: Cost Plus Pricing Exists to Eliminate Mid-Project Negotiation
teachings.explanation: The cost plus model was designed so that owners and builders are not constantly renegotiating during construction. The plus percentage should reflect the complexity of the project. When owners supply materials, the builder should still price as if they are buying everything to ensure full fees and overhead are covered regardless of who purchases what.
teachings.quote: The reason cost plus exists as a contract is because nobody wants to negotiate during the process. This is the cost. This is the percentage. You buy whatever you want to buy.

teachings.explanation: Rather than adding headcount to handle every function, keeping the team lean and cross-trained ensures coverage during absences, reduces overhead, and forces efficiency. This is especially important in a business that demands constant forward-thinking.
teachings.quote: Stay as lean as you can, as efficient as possible, have everyone cross-trained so that we can all cover for each other.

actionable_advice: Review your 12-month cash flow at least once a month — ideally weekly — so you always know your financial position before making hiring decisions.
To generate more work, contact feeders (architects, designers, realtors) directly in person rather than by email or DM alone.
When hiring, identify the exact expertise you need first, then find a candidate who also demonstrates an owner mindset and self-accountability.
Build project manager fees into each project contract so that the project itself covers the cost of the PM before you hire one.
Use a pencil-and-paper two-week rolling calendar, review it Monday and Friday with your PM, crumple it at week's end, and start fresh to build planning discipline.
Hold weekly Friday office hours where all project managers can gather, share problems, and learn from each other.
When visiting a job site, always look at ceiling, walls, and floor in each room and find at least 10 things to question or correct before leaving.
When a change affects a client's move-in date, call the client immediately and proactively — do not wait for them to ask.
At project closeout, give clients a week or two after move-in to compile their punch list in their notes app rather than calling you for every small item.
When setting client expectations on timeline, always add a month to whatever your internal estimate is before communicating a target date.
For owner-supplied materials on cost plus projects, price the full scope as if you are purchasing everything, then apply your fee to that full number regardless of who buys the materials.
Make sure clients providing their own materials understand the risk they carry for ordering errors, delays, and warranty complications that result from those materials.
Set up maintenance contracts with key trades (HVAC, plumber) on behalf of clients at handover, but have the owner sign and own those contracts directly.
client_challenges: David asked what personality traits and backgrounds the best project managers tend to share, and whether trades experience or administrative/production building experience matters more.
Renee asked how to know when to pull the trigger on hiring a full-time salaried employee, and what financial benchmarks or pipeline thresholds should signal readiness to scale.
Sydney struggled with communicating schedule changes and scope additions to clients throughout a project, especially when early delays compound and clients forget what was agreed months prior.
Patrick brought on a very green hire with no construction background and asked for systems or structured documents to help train someone from scratch.
Abby was wrapping up a custom project and needed guidance on warranty handover — including whether to provide a trade list and how to manage post-move-in client communication without constant texts.

Eric was approached for a large multi-structure compound project on a cost plus basis where the owner wants to supply some materials, and needed a framework for protecting full profitability when not all costs run through the builder.

Erica described a chaotic remodeling operation where planning is not happening, the team is not confirming materials before scheduling crews, and she is managing remotely from another city with poor team responsiveness.

Julie asked how to invest in continuous construction knowledge development for her project managers beyond organizational frameworks, noting there appears to be a gap in accessible, structured trade-level education for PMs.
notable_quotes: On urgency vs. reporting: 'The reporting of the systems are nice. They're nice. Homeowners like to see that. But the actual doing part of everything is more important.'
On facts over opinions: 'If that's a fact, you put it up there. If it's not a fact, I don't want your opinion. Go get the fact.'
On the construction business: 'This is not built for the weak. People will not survive this business if you're not constantly thinking ahead and positioning.'
On client accountability: 'My entire goal is they need to own their decisions. We're here to execute.'
On timeline buffering: 'Always, always, always add a month to whatever your superhero cape thinking brain thinks.'
On staying lean: 'There's no relief. And so for the money people, the hope of wanting to be relieved — I've just learned there's no relief.'
